texts.label: text
texts.orig: Rapid urbanisation, population growth, and rising industrialization, particularly in poorer nations have contributed to increasing humaninduced CO2  emissions. The world emits around 50 billion tonnes of greenhouse  gases  each  year,  and  this  amount  has  increased  50  fold since the mid-1800s with 73.2 % of emissions due to energy use (in industry 24.2 %, transport 16.2 %, and buildings 17.5 %, etc.), 18.4 % of agriculture and land use, with the remaining 8.4 % from direct industry (5.2 %, chemicals and cements) and waste (3.2 %, landfills and wastewater) [1]. Large-scale changes in global and national energy systems will be essential to reduce atmospheric CO2  levels. The IPCC Special Report on Global Warming of 1.5 ◦ C (SR1.5) launched in October 2018 outlines the impact of global warming of 1.5 ◦ C above pre-industrial levels. To meet the 1.5 ◦ C Paris target, global CO2  emissions will need to decline by 45 % from 2010 levels by 2030 and reach net zero by 2050 [2]. To achieve these targets, rapid and far-reaching transitions must occur during the coming decade -in energy, land, urban, and industrial systems. CO2 reduction scenarios will require significant improvements in  energy  efficiency,  the  commitment  by  Governments as  well  as  an extensive  rollout  of  zero-  or  low-carbon  energy  supply. texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas separation is a promising next-generation technology for post-combustion CO2  capture that can significantly reduce energy consumption, instrument, and chemical footprint relative to mature amine scrubbing that is in widespread use. Nevertheless, despite many studies commercially viable membranes are not widely implemented at a large scale. This review presents a systematic overview of the current status and advances of membrane technologies for CO2  capture from post-combustion flue gas. In particular, the potential for recent developments in membranes for scale-up based upon required performance parameters in these settings will be reviewed. The challenges and barriers to the upscale of membrane technologies are discussed with respect to both  materials  and  engineering  perspectives.  The  technology  readiness  level  of  various  technologies  and remaining barriers to their implementation are provided.

Iron, Steel, Cement, Pulp and Paper, and fossil fuelbased power generation Industries are among the large industrial sectors. Decarbonisation via post-combustion carbon capture is considered more important in these industries due to a lack of alternatives for future low-carbon  plants.  Liquid  amine  absorption  is  mature  and  has  been applied commercially for post-combustion carbon capture from these emission sites with high capture efficiency ( > 90 %) [4]. However, the disadvantages  of  a  significant  chemical  footprint  and  high  energy requirement  for  absorbent  regeneration  make  it  less  favorable  for further industrial installation. In recent years, membrane technologies for gas separation have gained increasing intention due to their high energy efficiency and small physical and chemical footprint. texts.orig: The  following  perspective  will  review  the  carbon  emissions,  and point sources from flue gases arranging from heavier CO2  emitting industry sectors, viz. Cement, Steel, and Pulp and Paper, to CO2 emission source in gas or coal-fired power stations focussing upon current postcombustion  technologies  for  mitigating  CO2  from  these  plants,  with particular attention paid to the latest developments in emerging membrane technologies for carbon capture. In particular, newly developed membrane materials/types will be discussed with respect to targeted membrane performance, the barriers to their upscale, such as materials and  engineering  process  challenges,  will  be  assessed  holistically  and include considerations such as the technology readiness level of CCS strategies and case studies for carbon capture from real flue gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 emissions from a plant may be dispersed over several sources, with  potentially  many  options  in  the  processing  pipeline  for  implementing CCS. Post-combustion capture is often common because it can be either retrofitted into existing plants or built as an end-of-pipe capture technology for a new plant. Retro-fitting can be very site-specific and will in most cases require integration and optimization specific to the requirements of the local site conditions. The challenge for each of these sectors will be maintaining competitiveness upon implementation of CCS. The following sections briefly review the processes involved in each  industry  sector  of  interest  and  current  practices  for  CCS,  in particular with respect to post-combustion CCS.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2.1. CCS in Iron and Steel industry
texts.text: 2.2.1. CCS in Iron and Steel industry

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Iron and Steel production is an energy and emissions-intensive process. This sector is the largest industrial source of direct CO2  emission, representing about 25 -30 % of industrial emissions. In 2020, direct CO2 emissions from this sector accounted for ~ 9 % of all global anthropogenic CO2 emissions (2.6 billion tonnes) with an average of 1.85 t CO2 emitted for every tonne of steel produced [19,30]. The main manufacturing route is based upon an integrated steel mill with an oxygen blast furnace (BF-BOF). This approach accounts for up to ~ 75 % of all global steelmaking, with an energy intensity of ~ 21 -23 GJ/t. The greatest potential for reducing emissions and carbon capture is therefore in integrated steel mills with large point sources. In recent decades, this sector has achieved significant reductions in energy consumption and improved  efficiency  by  employing  the  best  available  technologies (BATs). Further efficiency improvements alone cannot meet CO2  emissions  targets.  Hence,  carbon  capture  is  deemed  essential  to  enable additional reductions required in this sector [19,30].

texts.orig: emissions) in an integrated steel mill, many studies have focused upon conventional post-combustion retrofitting of capture technology at the blast furnace. A typical composition of gases from the BF is CO2 (17 -25 %), CO (20 -28 %), H2 (1 -5 %), N2 (50 -55 %) at an operating pressure of ~ 2 -3 bar [20]. CO2 capture from the blast furnace is achieved either by 1) direct capture, 2) capture after conversion of CO to CO2  using the water gas shift reaction, or 3) modification of the blast furnace for top gas recycling. Post-combustion amine capture of blast furnace emissions can capture CO2  emissions at a cost of $65.1 -119.2 per tonne and has the potential to mitigate 50 -75 % of emissions [31]. CO2  removal has been tested at pilot scale for top gas recycling using chemical absorption with  monoethanolamine  (MEA)  or  physical  absorption  (selexol)  and vacuum pressure swing adsorption (VSPA). Top gas recycling has been investigated in the European Union as part of the Ultra-Low CO2 Steelmaking consortium (ULCOS) [3].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several CO2  capture technologies have been explored and are presented in Table 1. Most commonly, chemical absorption is useful for blast furnaces with low partial pressure, whilst pressure swing adsorption  (PSA)  or  vapor  swing  absorption  require  relatively  higher  CO2 partial pressures [12,20,32]. PSA is a cyclic adsorption process, which allows continuous separation of gas streams, and is considered viable for separation of CO2 from flue gases containing about 5 -15 % v/v [33]. For example, pressure swing absorption (PSA) technology is applied in tail gas treatment in smelting reduction (e.g., blast furnaces. FINEX technology has been applied to medium commercial scale operation. The PSA unit at Posco ' s Finex ® plant in South Korea removes CO2 from the fluidised bed reactors off gas to produce a 3 % CO2  containing gas for recycling back to the reactors [19,32,34].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2.2. CCS in the C ement industry
texts.text: 2.2.2. CCS in the C ement industry

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Cement  production  is  the  second  most  CO2-intensive  industrial process, accounting for 2.2 GtCO2/year worldwide, which is ~ 5 -7 % of the  global  anthropogenic  CO2  emissions  [24,35].  CO2  emissions  are approximately  0.9 -1.0  t/t  cement  (energy  and  feedstock  combined), with ~ 60 % of the CO2  originating from the calcination of the raw material carbonate or limestone (CaCO3) to calcium oxide (CaO). The CO2 concentration in cement plant flue gas is generally around 20 -30 %. Benhelal has summarized several recent studies exploring potential CO2 mitigation strategies from cement plants, with many of these focused on fuel/energy and the use of alternative materials; relatively few  have considered carbon capture [23]. However, there have been some reports proposing post-combustion capture using amine scrubbing with monoethanolamine (MEA) [21] as an option that can be implemented with low technical risk in the short-term [3,21-23,36,37]. Other CO2 capture technologies, such as oxy-firing the kiln and calcium looping, have also been investigated and were observed to be less expensive than MEA, though they were less developed. Large scale testing and pilot plant infrastructure are being explored currently in this sector using amine solutions  (Aker  Solutions);  solid  sorbents  (RTI  International);  membranes (DNV); calcium-looping (ITRI) and oxygen-firing (ECRA) [38].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2.3. CCS in the Pulp and Paper industry
texts.text: 2.2.3. CCS in the Pulp and Paper industry

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Pulp and Paper industry contributes ~ 2 % of the total industrial global emissions, equivalent to 252 million tonnes of CO2 per year [39]. The two most common production routes are mechanical mills and integrated Kraft mills, with the process categorised as two distinct processes: pulping and paper making. Kraft Mills dominate production and offer the most opportunity for carbon capture. There are several emission sources and the largest emission source in an integrated Kraft Pulp and Paper plant tends to be the recovery boiler where black liquor is combusted. Post-combustion CO2  capture from recovery boilers using MEA absorption is a potential pathway for CCS, though there have been very few studies of CCS in the Pulp and Paper industry [3,27,40]. The retrofit of CCS in the Pulp and Paper industry will increase the cost of pulp in the absence of carbon incentives. As with the other sectors discussed, the feasibility of CCS in this sector is strongly dependent on how CO2 emissions are categorized and incentivized [41]. Furthermore, due to the requirement of these mills to be close to the source (i.e., forests), their location is often remote, and therefore CCS in the Pulp and Paper industry  is  often  overlooked  in  terms  of  carbon  networks  and  infrastructure integration [3].
texts.text: The Pulp and Paper industry contributes ~ 2 % of the total industrial global emissions, equivalent to 252 million tonnes of CO2 per year [39]. The two most common production routes are mechanical mills and integrated Kraft mills, with the process categorised as two distinct processes: pulping and paper making. Kraft Mills dominate production and offer the most opportunity for carbon capture. There are several emission sources and the largest emission source in an integrated Kraft Pulp and Paper plant tends to be the recovery boiler where black liquor is combusted. Post-combustion CO2  capture from recovery boilers using MEA absorption is a potential pathway for CCS, though there have been very few studies of CCS in the Pulp and Paper industry [3,27,40]. The retrofit of CCS in the Pulp and Paper industry will increase the cost of pulp in the absence of carbon incentives. As with the other sectors discussed, the feasibility of CCS in this sector is strongly dependent on how CO2 emissions are categorized and incentivized [41]. Furthermore, due to the requirement of these mills to be close to the source (i.e., forests), their location is often remote, and therefore CCS in the Pulp and Paper industry  is  often  overlooked  in  terms  of  carbon  networks  and  infrastructure integration [3].

texts.orig: 2.3. Comparison of technologies for Post-combustion CO2  capture
texts.text: 2.3. Comparison of technologies for Post-combustion CO2  capture

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A host of technologies are being developed at various technology readiness  levels  (TRL).  This  is  a  nine-point  scaling  system  used  to qualitatively evaluate technology maturity level through various phases of  research  and  development  (R & D)  where:  TRL1-Concept,  TRL2Formulation, TRL3-Proof of Concept (lab tests), TRL4-Lab Prototype, TRL5-Lab  Scale  Plant,  TRL6-Pilot  Scale,  TRL7-Demonstration,  TRL8Commercial Refinement Required, and TRL9-Commercial [38]. Table 2 summarises the TRL of technologies developed to date together with their advantages and challenges [46]. The technologies for CO2 capture or  separation  that  are  sufficiently  developed  and  implemented  at  a commercial level (TRL9) are absorption and cryogenic methodologies. Absorption  can  handle  low  CO2  concentration  using  aqueous  amine scrubbing  and  achieve  high  CO2 capture  efficiency.  The  cryogenic method is suitable for high CO2 concentration ( > 90 %) and yields a high purity liquified CO2 product. Both technologies are energy-intensive. In contrast, membrane gas separation technology achieving CO2  separation using a partial pressure driving force is potential to be more energyefficient  than  the  aforementioned  methodologies  when  treating  CO2 feed content > 10 % coupled with a fine tuning of vacuum pumping controlled driving force. It is also worth noting that a direct comparison of energy consumption between amine absorption and membrane separation  technologies  is  difficult  due  to  the  different  energy  source required (electricity for membrane and heat for absorption). Membrane separation does not involve hazardous chemical storage, handling, or disposal, but they have lower separation efficiency than those mature technologies. The highest TRL level for CO2 capture from flue gas using membranes is currently at pilot-scale (TRL-6). The challenges to scale-up are discussed in the following sections.

texts.text: 3. Membrane technology for carbon capture and sequestration

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane technology has received increasing attention in the past few decades for carbon capture and sequestration due to its advantages of high energy efficiency (no phase change required) as well as small physical and chemical footprints [47]. It has been applied to the industry for hydrogen recovery (first in the mid-1970 s, for ammonia purge gas separation), air separation, and natural gas sweetening (to meet pipeline specifications, < 2 % CO2) [5]. Currently, membranes are considered a second-generation methodology to capture/separate CO2 from its mixtures. A study by Baker et al. in 2018 demonstrated the potential of membranes for post-combustion carbon capture. This study showed a greater potential for more reduced energy consumption relative to the commonly used amine absorption when CO2 feed content was > 10 %. By normalizing the energy required at 10 % CO2 feed content as 1, there is a Δ 44 % more drop of energy use marked by membrane separation (52 % drop) comparing with amine absorption technology (7.5 % drop) at CO2  feed content up to 30 %, which yields > 99 % CO2  purity in the product stream (Fig. 2) [12]. texts.orig: the DOE ' s Updated Cost Report (August 2012) also demonstrated less energy consumption for CO2  capture in membrane process than in the amine absorption process (358 kWh/tonne CO2 vs. 382 kWh/tonne CO2) through a direct comparison by plants that were sized to produce about 550 MWe net power output with CO2 emission of 608 tonne/h and fixed CO2  capture  efficiency  at  90  %)  [48,49].  Another  simulation  study showed that a membrane with CO2 permeance of 1000 GPU and a CO2/ N2  selectivity of 50 using air as the sweep gas could meet DOE CO2 capture targets ( > 90 % recovery at 40$/ton CO2  captured) for postcombustion carbon capture [50].  This  result  highlights  the  potential for  membrane technology in other sectors, since most industrial CO2 emissions contain input streams with CO2  concentrations > 10 % (e.g., electric power plants (12 -15   mol %), cement production (20 -30 mol %), refineries (30 -40 mol %), and iron and steel plants (20 -30 % mol) [12]. However, challenges still remain in the practical application of membranes for post-combustion carbon capture (TRL9-Commercial). In the following sections, we summarize the recent development of membrane technology for post-combustion CO2 capture and separation from TRL1 (Concept) to TRL6 (Pilot-scale). These results include promising membrane  materials  and  types,  challenges/gaps  from  materials  and engineering aspects, and case studies of membrane application for CO2 capture and sequestration from flue gas on a pilot scale.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Membrane materials
texts.text: 3.1. Membrane materials

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To achieve high CO2  separation efficiency, a membrane must have high permeance, high selectivity, thinness, and also physical, chemical, mechanical,  and  thermal  stability  for  industrial  consideration.  Polymeric materials are favored for their good processibility, solubility in common solvents, and low cost relative to inorganic materials. Despite their generally high chemical and thermal stability, inorganic materials have drawbacks such as poor reproductivity, brittleness, and high cost. Furthermore, the easy processability of polymer materials into membrane modules with high membrane area to volume ratio has significantly increased their  popularity  [5,51].  The  industry-dominated membrane materials for gas separation are mainly rubbery polymers (poly(dimethylsiloxane) and ethylene oxide/propylene oxide -amide copolymers)  and  glassy  polymers  (cellulose  acetate,  tetra  bromo bisphenol A (e.g., Matrimid, P84), polycarbonate, polyimides, poly (2,6 dimethyl phenylene oxide), and polysulfone) [5]. For carbon capture from post-combustion flue gas, suitable membrane materials are rare, so there have been only three membranes demonstrated at pilot scale for post-combustion CO2 capture (cf. Table 3 ) .

texts.orig: We summarized recently developed polymeric membrane materials (Table 4) and their utilization in various membrane types including thin film composite (TFC, Table 5), fixed site carrier (FSC, Table 6) membrane, and gas -liquid membrane contactor (MC) for CCS from flue gas including both lab-scale and pilot-scale/case studies. These membrane performance  was  evaluated  by  comparing  it  with  the  2008  CO2/N2 upper bound in Fig. 10 [38]. Potentially promising polymeric materials for scale-up are considered to be those with CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity breaking the upper bound. As shown in Table 4 and Fig. 10, none of the free-standing polymeric membrane at lab-scale (TRL ≦ 3) with  thickness > 10 μ m  can  break  2008  Robeson  upper  bound  after normalization  from  permeability  (Barrer)  to  permeance  (GPU).  In contrast, the thin film composite membrane by coating a thin selective layer on support usually offer higher CO2  gas separation performance which was discussed in the following section.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From a low-cost perspective, thin-film composite membrane structures  with  multilayer  composites  (1 -2  g/m 2 )  use  much  less  material compared to traditional asymmetric membranes (50 g/m 2 ) (Fig. 3 ) . The gas transport behavior is via solution-diffusion (SD) under the driving force of a partial pressure difference between the membrane feed and permeate sides. The gas molecule is first adsorbed on the membrane surface (step 1) and dissolved into the bulk of the membrane, then diffuses  through the membrane (step 2), and finally desorbed from the other side of the membrane (step 3).

Practically, a non-ideal interface at the boundary layer between the polymer and additives is a common behavior for MMMs with interfacial voids (Fig. 4 , Bottom a ), rigidified polymer chain layers (Fig. 4 , Bottom b ), or pore blockage regions (Fig. 4 , Bottom c ). Interfacial voids are caused by insufficient affinity between the polymer and additive and usually lead to  increased  membrane  permeability  due  to  increased  gas  transport pathways. Selectivity can be decreased or increased depending on the size  of  the  voids  created  at  the  interface.  Rigidified  polymer  chains originate from the immobilization of polymer chains on the nanoparticle surface  due  to  strong  interactions  and,  generally,  this  results  in decreased permeability. Pore blocking is caused by the polymer chains partially obstructing a part of the pores of the embedded nanoparticles in  the  polymer  matrix.  Typically,  it  enhances  membrane  selectivity, despite being accompanied by reduced permeability. Taking account of these factors, experimental results for MMM usually deviate from prediction of the Maxwell model. texts.orig: Among these TFC membranes in Table 5, Polaris is a widely studied and evaluated membrane for CO2 capture from coal-based power station flue  gas  by  Membrane  Technology  and  Research  (MTR)  [43-45]. Polaris TM Gen 1, with a CO2  permeance of 1000 GPU and a CO2/N2 selectivity  of  50,  has  reached  commercial  scale.  Between  2012  and 2015, spiral-wound modules showed stable membrane performance > 111,000 h with over 90 % CO2 capture at bench scale (0.05 MWe or 1 tonne CO2/day). A scaled-up small pilot unit (1 MWe or 20 tonnes CO2/ day) also showed stable performance over 6 months and achieved up to 90 % CO2  capture. A low-pressure-drop plate-and-frame module, with higher packing density and lower power cost (~10 Mwe saving) relative to spiral-wound module, was successfully demonstrated at the 20 TPD pilot scale. Polaris Gen 2 with 2 × CO2 permeance and similar CO2/N2 selectivity shows 60-70 % more CO2 removal capacity than that of the Gen 1 and was tested at bench scale with stable performance over 40 h of cumulative run time. Such efforts from MTR made membrane technology for CO2  capture from flue gas advance from TRL2/3 to TRL 6. A large pilot-scale of 200 TPD (tonne CO2/day) from a coal-fired power plant  is  currently  under  design  and  planned  installation  in  the  year 2021 -2022 with an expected > 99 % CO2 purity for EOR. A large pilot-scale of 200 TPD (tonne CO2/day) from a coal-fired power plant  is  currently  under  design  and  planned  installation  in  the  year 2021 -2022 with an expected > 99 % CO2 purity for EOR. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fixed-site-carrier (FSC) membranes are another class of membranes that have been demonstrated at the pilot scale (TRL 5 -6) for CO2 capture from flue gas ( Section 3.1) . They exhibit high CO2/N2  selectivity and low energy efficiency penalty (10 %) compared to conventional amine absorption [52,81]. FSC membranes have a similar membrane structure to  a  thin-film  composite as  shown in Fig. 5, with a fixed-site carrier incorporated into a functional layer designed for CO2 gas transport. In FSC membrane, the carrier for CO2 is usually an amino-functional group ( -NH2) covalently bound to a polymer matrix. Feed gas containing CO2 can reversibly react with -NH2 groups in the membrane to form intermediate NH3 + and HCO3 -ions with sufficient water, then diffuse through the membrane and deliver CO2 during the regeneration process due to the low CO2 partial pressure (Fig. 5) [82]. texts.orig: from flue gas. As a result, CO2  transport through FSC membranes is a combination of facilitated transport (FT) and solution-diffusion (SD), whereas the non-reactive N2  gas will only permeate via SD. In some cases,  additional mobile carriers are added to the polymer matrix to form MMMs that will improve CO2 transport. Consequently, a desirable CO2  separation performance from flue gas generally can be achieved through FSC membranes. We have summarized recently reported materials for FSC membranes from lab-scale to pilot scale (Table 6). The performance  of  these  membranes  was  compared  with  2008  Robeson upper bound [63,64,83,84]. As can be seen in Table 6 and Fig. 10, some FSC  membranes  show  promising  potential  for  flue  gas  treatment  by breaking  2008 Robeson upper bound. These mainly were FSC membranes  with  extra  mobile  carrier  such  as  amino-containing  polymers with  mobile  carrier  of  Lewis  acidic  water,  piperazine  glycinate,  or MWCNTs.  Besides,PEO/CF3CO2K  membranes  with  a  mobile  carrier (Lewis acidic water) showed good stability over 6 months and MWCNT embedded PNVF-co-VAm FSC membrane showed stable performance up to 4 bar. Furthermore, in process simulations, the combination of water vapor as a sweep gas and vacuum pumps is more economical than the use of vacuum pumps only for flue gas treatment at a coal-fired power plant, leading to 90 % recovery and 95 % CO2 purity [85].Before pilot scale  operation,  the  possibility  of  fixed-site-carrier  membrane  (FSC) technology  for  post-combustion  CO2  capture  was  demonstrated  in  a simulation study in 2009 to give 90 % purity of CO2 and 90 % recovery by using polyvinylamine (PVAm) membrane. This membrane system was later applied to pre-pilot demonstration (Flat sheet, single stage, 1.5 m 2 membrane area) in Norway under real flue gas conditions (coal-fired power plant, in the year of 2013). It demonstrated with CO2 permeance of cal. 220 GPU, CO2/N2 selectivity of 300, and good stability over 6.5 months  [86].  In  the  year  of  2017,  the  same  PVAm  membrane  was upgraded from flat sheet to hollow fiber configuration with membrane area of 4 -18 m 2 on a pilot scale showing stable performance of 60 -83 mol % CO2 purity over 6 months even under a harsh environment with SO2  and  NOX  impurities  [87].  Some  pre-pilot  plant  tests  were  also conducted  using  fixed  site  carrier  membrane  with  additional  mobile carriers, recently. For example, a field test for CO2 capture from cement plant has been operated by using PVA/ProK hybrid membrane (hollow fibre module, ~ 200 cm 2 membrane area) with up to 40 wt % amino acid salt. CO2 flux of ~ 5 × 10 3 cm 3 (STP)cm 2 s 1 and 50 % CO2 purity were documented (exceed 2008 Robeson upper bound) [88]. Another prepilot study also showed CO2  flux of 750 NL m -2 h 1 and with a purity of 50 -55 vol % CO2  by using hybrid facilitated transport membranes (HFTM,  i.e.,  polyallylamine  (SHPAA)/polyvinyl  alcohol  (PVA)/graphene oxide (pGO)) with a 10 % [Emim][OAc] loading and showed good membrane performance over two weeks [89].

texts.orig: 3.4. Membrane contactors
texts.text: 3.4. Membrane contactors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A comparable energy consumption can be expected for membrane technology  relative  to  amine  absorption  method  when  treated  with concentrated  CO2 feed  gas  because  the  partial  pressure  generated driving force is highly sensitive to CO2 concentration, as shown in Fig. 2. This benefit favors membranes in cement, iron and steel production, and refineries (CO2  emission > 10 mol %). However, when CO2 concentrations are < 10 % in the feed gas, membrane gas separation technology loses  much  of  its  advantages.  This  gap  makes  gas -liquid  membrane contactors (MC) viable for handling low post-combustion CO2  concentration in industrial applications such as in natural gas (3 -10 vol %) and petroleum-fired power plants(3 -8 vol %).[99] Compared to membrane gas separation technology, for which CO2 selectivity is highly dependent on the membrane materials, in a gas -liquid membrane contactor, the porous membrane is used only to provide a gas and liquid phase contact interface that avoids direct mixing. Ideally, membrane pores are filled with gas rather than liquid (pore wetting). CO2  selectivity depends on the gas phase species solubility in the selected liquid absorbents (Fig. 6). [100,101].

texts.orig: used in MC, with liquid flow in the core and gas flow on the shell side to increase contact time. As shown in Fig. 7, CO2  in flue gas containing other gases (e.g., N2  and H2O) is absorbed by the counter-current lean solvent when they meet at the two-phase interfaces (i.e., the pores in the membrane). The rich solvent after absorption is regenerated by applying vacuum or other methods to release the CO2. The regenerated solvent will be recycled back to treat additional flue gas. An experimental study has demonstrated that a higher interfacial area, as in the case of MC, can have a 40 -50 % enhancement of overall adsorption capacity relative to a packed column, which is comparable to a 40 -50 % decrease in size [101].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane material, absorbent liquid, and process conditions (e.g., temperature and pressure) in MC will affect CO2 capture efficiency. For example, membrane materials need to have good compatibility with the liquid absorbents and strong resistance to contaminants to avoid membrane  wetting,  degradation,  and  fouling.  In  MC,  porous  polymeric membranes  mainly  include polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), Polyethylene (PE), and polyether ether ketone (PEEK). On the other hand, inorganic membranes are typically ceramic membranes due to their high thermal and chemical stability.[103,104] The liquid phase is usually an alkali solution with various  CO2 absorbents. For further details of  membrane  type, absorbents type, MC challenges, interested readers can refer to other review papers.[88,100,105,106].

texts.orig: Advanced glassy polymers with higher fractional free volume (FFV) relative to conventional low FFV polymers have been explored to disrupt the  trade-off  between  permeability  and  selectivity  (Table  4 ) .  Some membranes demonstrated their potential for CO2 capture from flue gas by exceeding the CO2/N2  upper bound. Crosslinking and MMMs have proven promising to mitigate/stop physical aging and plasticization issues.[107]  The  effect  of  crosslinking  via  thermal,  chemical,  and  UV methods, as well as photo-oxidation on membrane long-term stability, are summarized in Table 7. Crosslinking stabilizes membrane performance through chemical bond formation between functional groups in the polymer structure or with the external crosslinker. Photo-oxidation slows  down  membrane  physical  aging  by  polymer  chain-scission, which  reduces  the  driving  force  (excess  volume)  of  physical  aging through dense layer formation after UV irradiation. Further details of crosslinking that mitigate plasticization can be found elsewhere.[108]

texts.orig: On the other hand, two key factors that affect long-term stability in MMMs are the interaction at the polymer-nanoparticle interface and nanoparticle dispersion behavior in a polymer matrix. The embedded nanoparticle size, loading amount, and solvent used are important factors. Generally, a close interaction at the polymer-additive boundary is associated with more stabilized overall membrane performance (antiaging or anti-plasticization). The close interaction is usually accompanied by reduced polymer chain entanglement through polymer chain adsorption  on  nanoparticle  surfaces  or  threading  into  the  pores  of nanoparticles (Fig. 9). The reduction of polymer chain entanglement through the inclusion of nanoparticles can be characterized by a reduced composite  polymer  solution  viscosity  change.  Therefore,  viscosity change due to nanoparticle addition can provide a rough prediction of composite  membrane  performance.  A  reduced  viscosity  is  usually associated  with  a  relatively  homogenous  nanoparticle  dispersion, improved membrane selectivity, and reduced physical aging; whereas increased viscosity is accompanied by nanoparticle agglomeration and accelerated physical aging. If there is no obvious viscosity change, other characterization methods are needed to rationalize membrane performance, such as PALS, SEM, solid 13 C NMR. Further details regarding this topic can be found elsewhere [107].
texts.text: On the other hand, two key factors that affect long-term stability in MMMs are the interaction at the polymer-nanoparticle interface and nanoparticle dispersion behavior in a polymer matrix. The embedded nanoparticle size, loading amount, and solvent used are important factors. Generally, a close interaction at the polymer-additive boundary is associated with more stabilized overall membrane performance (antiaging or anti-plasticization). The close interaction is usually accompanied by reduced polymer chain entanglement through polymer chain adsorption  on  nanoparticle  surfaces  or  threading  into  the  pores  of nanoparticles (Fig. 9). The reduction of polymer chain entanglement through the inclusion of nanoparticles can be characterized by a reduced composite  polymer  solution  viscosity  change.  Therefore,  viscosity change due to nanoparticle addition can provide a rough prediction of composite  membrane  performance.  A  reduced  viscosity  is  usually associated  with  a  relatively  homogenous  nanoparticle  dispersion, improved membrane selectivity, and reduced physical aging; whereas increased viscosity is accompanied by nanoparticle agglomeration and accelerated physical aging. If there is no obvious viscosity change, other characterization methods are needed to rationalize membrane performance, such as PALS, SEM, solid 13 C NMR. Further details regarding this topic can be found elsewhere [107].

texts.orig: The barrier to industrial/commercial use of such membranes for CO2 capture/separation is in the up-scaling of these membranes such that they  are  defect-free  to  retain  their  gas  separation  performance  and exhibit good thermal and mechanical stability in a real flue gas stream. Significant attention should be paid to the interface between the polymer and additives in a composite membrane, since the interface plays a vital role in overall membrane performance, especially when membrane thickness is reduced, since defects are more easily created.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to membrane materials, the engineering process is the other key factor limiting practical application of membranes for CO2 capture. Pressure is a vital element during the engineering process for gas separation in membranes. A greater pressure difference across the membrane will result  in  a  higher  gas  flux.  However,  a  low-pressure driving force is a critical issue for post-combustion carbon capture due to the low CO2 content in the flue gas. According to Baker et al., the most practical CO2 partial pressure ratio is a maximum of 10, corresponding to  2  bar  feed  pressure  and  a  0.2  bar  permeate  pressure.  Under  such conditions, with a fixed CO2 recovery at 90 % and CO2 feed content at 11.6 %, these authors have shown through simulation that membrane selectivity  of > 50  (e.g.,  200)  is  not  necessary,  since  there  is  no remarkable improvement in CO2 product purity (from 0.46 to 0.55, an increase of 20 %) at a cost of enormous membrane area (from 2 to 58 MMm 2 , a 28-fold increase).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  pressure  ratio/driving  force  can  be  obtained  either  by  feed compression or by adding vacuum to the permeate side. Despite much less membrane area (3.6-fold less) by feed compression (up to 5 bar), simulations show a better economy in the latter scenario (i.e., permeate side vacuum requires much less energy (80 % less)). This assertion is based upon simulations of a one-stage membrane process with a flue gas composition of 13 % CO2, 87 % N2, and no O2, no H2O from a 600 MWe power plant and recovering 90 % of the CO2. The simulation also shows that counter-flow has the potential to save membrane area by 38 %, energy consumption by 18 %, and enhance permeate CO2 concentration by 12 % relative to cross flow. Furthermore, introducing the residue gas as sweep gas at the permeate side in a counter-flow module at 6 % flow of the feed gas allows for a further reduction in membrane area (40 %). This is due to the sweep gas (mostly nitrogen) diluting the permeate CO2 to increase the CO2  driving force across the membrane. When a twostage  counter-flow  module  coupled  with  sweep  gas  was  applied,  the simulation showed a more energy-efficient manner to reach the same product quality ( > 95 % CO2  product concentration). The total power saved was 49 %, with a reduction in membrane area of 130 %, and the cost of capture ($/ton CO2) was reduced by 70 % compared to a twostage permeate vacuum system.[50] Reduced power consumption was also shown in other simulated or experimental studies using selective exhaust gas recycling.[8,12,116,117] Other workers have simulated a three-step membrane plant in Nanjing, China with expected targets of 95 % CO2 product purity and 90 % CO2 capture.[118].

texts.text: 4.1. Materials

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thin-film-composite,  fixed-site-carrier  membrane,  and  gas -liquid membrane contactors are promising membrane  types for postcombustion  CO2  capture.  However,  each  of  these  has  its  own  challenges to scale-up. Among these membranes including at lab and pilot scales, the fixed-site-carrier membranes showed desirable performance by both high CO2  flux and CO2/N2  selectivity crossing 2008 Robeson upper bound (Fig. 10), a higher capacity for CO2 carriers in either fixed or mobile form is expected to further improve its separation performance. Compared with FSC membranes, thin-film composite and the  free-standing  polymeric  membranes  with  inferior  CO2  separation performance are mainly limited by the trade-off between permeance and selectivity. There is a need to develop materials with improved stability, including  polymer  material  in  FSC  and  TFC  membranes  with  high resistance to contaminants such as SOx  and NOx  in flue gas and high wetting  resistance  polymeric  membrane  in  Membrane  contactor,  to close to the practical lifetime target of 3 -5 years. Scale-up fabrication and  evaluation  of  promising  functional  and  substrate  materials  that retain properties are highly encouraged to shorten the gap from lab to pilot levels.

texts.orig: CCS Value Chain- Carbon Hubs and Clusters : There is a need for an improved  CCS  technology  value  chain  that  will  allow  for  successful integration  of  three  major  component  technologies:  CO2 capture, transportation,  and  storage  at  large-scale  (commercial)  projects.  The Global Climate Change and Sequestration Institute reported 65 industrial carbon capture and utilisation hubs in 2020 that are in operation or development. The CCS facilities currently in operation can capture and permanently store around 40 Mt of CO2 p/annum.
